metadata.title: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = superposition / sloshing / dynamic; CRIMSON = eigenstate / still / frozen
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no ⟨x⟩(t) cross-integral, no product-to-sum trig, no femtosecond numerics. Wonder register: beat-frequency sloshing from first principles.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Each energy state in a box sits perfectly still — its probability density never moves. Add two of them together and suddenly the probability sloshes wall to wall. Two frozen things make one moving thing.

card.copy: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One

beats.narration_text: The ground state: one hump in the center, perfectly still. The first excited state: two humps, one on each side, also perfectly still. Both are stationary states — their |ψ|² never shifts.

production_viz.label: n=1 and n=2 eigenstates — each probability density frozen

beats.narration_text: Mix them — equal parts ground state and first excited state. The probability cloud starts to rock back and forth, left to right, rhythmically. Both components were frozen. The sum is not. Why?

card.copy: Two stationary states. Each probability frozen. Add them: the probability sloshes wall to wall. How do two still things make something that moves?

beats.narration_text: Each eigenstate is a spinning clock in the complex plane. State one spins at rate E-one over h-bar. State two spins faster, at E-two over h-bar. The clocks start together and drift apart.

production_viz.label: two clock hands spinning at ω₁ and ω₂ — drifting apart

beats.narration_text: The combined probability density depends on the angle between the two clocks. As the angle grows from zero to pi, the probability shifts to the left. As it returns to zero, the probability shifts to the right. The period is set by the difference in rotation rates.

production_viz.label: angle between clocks drives the sloshing — period = 2π/(ω₂−ω₁)

beats.narration_text: When the clocks are aligned, one side of the box gets the probability. When they are opposite, the other side does. The cloud sloshes at the Bohr frequency — E-two minus E-one, divided by h-bar.

The total energy does not change — it remains a mixture of the two eigenstate energies. The sloshing is motion in space, not energy. Energy is not definite in the superposition; position is where the beat shows up.

production_viz.label: energy distribution fixed; position probability oscillates

beats.narration_text: This is the quantum version of a beat note in acoustics. Two pure tones close in frequency combine into a slowly pulsing envelope. The Bohr frequency plays the role of the beat frequency — same mathematics, different scale.

production_viz.label: acoustic beat note analogy — two tones → pulsing envelope

beats.narration_text: In hydrogen, a superposition of n-equals-1 and n-equals-2 sloshes at about 2.5 times 10 to the 15 hertz — roughly the frequency of ultraviolet light. The electron oscillates between the two shells at that rate.

production_viz.label: H atom n=1+n=2 superposition: beat ≈ 2.5×10¹⁵ Hz (illustrative)

beats.narration_text: Each eigenstate is frozen. Their phases spin at different rates. The angle between them grows and returns at the beat frequency. That beat is what makes the probability slosh.

card.copy: Each eigenstate frozen. Phases spin at different rates. The difference is the beat. The beat makes the probability slosh.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
